data.title: China and France agree to work together on nuclear issues as Macron wraps up visit
data.description:
China and France vowed to expand cooperation on nuclear issues and global challenges as French President Emmanuel Macron wrapped up his three-day visit to China on Friday.
They reaffirmed their commitment to “promoting the safe development of nuclear energy and strengthening collaboration across the nuclear energy industry chain”, state broadcaster CCTV reported.
The two nuclear powers also expressed their support for non-proliferation and underscored the key role of the International Atomic Energy Agency in building a strong and sustainable global framework for nuclear safety and security.
A separate statement highlighted discussions between the two countries on global conflicts, including Ukraine – one of the main issues Macron raised when he met Chinese President Xi Jinping on Thursday – and Gaza.
In a brief line, the statement said China and France “support all efforts to achieve a ceasefire and restore peace on the basis of international law and the purposes and principles of the UN Charter”.
It echoed the message Xi delivered to Macron on Thursday, when he said China supported “all efforts conducive to peace and will continue to play a constructive role in the political settlement of the crisis in its own way”.
On Gaza, they stressed the need to create favourable conditions for the effective implementation of the “two-state solution”, which was described as “the only credible solution to meet the legitimate expectations of Israelis and Palestinians for a just and lasting peace and security”.
They also urged all parties to fulfil their commitments to the ceasefire agreement reached in October.
“As permanent members of the UN Security Council, China and France are jointly committed to seeking constructive solutions based on international law to address the challenges and threats to international security and stability,” the statement said.
“Both countries reaffirmed their willingness to maintain close communication on issues concerning international peace and security.”
Other joint statements included pledges to cooperate on agriculture and on tackling climate and environmental challenges.

Another statement said Xi and Macron had “called for the continued promotion of multilateralism”, adding that Paris appreciated global governance initiatives proposed by Beijing.
It said: “Multilateralism is the cornerstone of the international order and the best way to promote international cooperation, maintain world peace and prosperity, and address the ever-emerging common problems and challenges.”
The joint statements were released on the final day of Macron’s three-day trip to China – his fourth visit as president – which included another meeting with Xi, this time in the southwestern province of Sichuan.
In a rare gesture, the Chinese leader showed Macron an ancient dam that formed part of the Dujiangyan irrigation system that was built in 256 BC and expanded in 141 BC.
Dujiangyan, seen as the earliest oldest human-made irrigation system still in operation, was made a Unesco World Heritage site in 2000.
The Chinese leader also referred to his trip to France last year, where Macron took him to a part of the Pyrenees where he spent his childhood holidays.
“Last year you invited me to your hometown in the Hautes-Pyrenees, and I believe this trip will further enrich your understanding of China”, Xi said, according to CCTV.
Xi said the irrigation system was one of the earliest examples of harmonious coexistence between humanity and nature, and its construction reflected the Chinese nation’s spirit of self-reliance and perseverance.
“The French nation also possesses a tenacious spirit. China and France should be able to understand and respect each other better than other countries,” he said.
“Faced with a complex and volatile international situation, it is believed that China and France, two great historical and cultural powers, can make greater contributions to world peace and stability and the progress of humankind through dialogue and cooperation.”
The report added that Macron had said France was willing to deepen communication and coordination with China and work together to safeguard world peace and stability.
On Thursday, the two leaders held closed-door talks in Beijing, which Xi described as “friendly, candid and fruitful”, saying the two countries “should understand and support each other on issues involving each other’s core interests and major concerns”.
Macron said after the talks that there would be disagreements between the two sides “but we have a responsibility to overcome them”.
He also called on China to join European efforts to achieve a ceasefire in Ukraine, saying it was “crucial that we work together for a just and lasting peace, one that respects international law”.
The two leaders also witnessed the signing of 12 agreements that covered issues ranging from higher education and nuclear energy to panda conservation.
Macron was visiting China at a time of heightened tensions between the European Union and China. Brussels is particularly concerned about Beijing’s stance on the Ukraine war because of its close ties with Russia, its control of critical mineral supplies and manufacturing overcapacity.
When European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen visited China in July, she said relations between the bloc and China were at an “inflection point”.

data.title: Taipei protests after South Korea tourist arrival card lists Taiwan as ‘China (Taiwan)’
data.description:
South Korea has designated Taiwan as “China (Taiwan)” on its online registration form for arriving tourists, visitors from the island recently discovered.
This comes as Beijing and Tokyo are locked in a diplomatic spat over the Japanese prime minister’s remarks on a hypothetical Taiwan contingency.
Taiwan has protested the “unfriendly” designation, with its foreign ministry on Wednesday citing multiple reports from Taiwanese travelling to South Korea and urging that Seoul correct the “apparently factually incorrect” label.
On the website for South Korea’s electronic arrival form, known as the e-arrival card, both the “previous departure place” and “next destination” fields on the drop-down menu list Taiwan as “China (Taiwan)”.
Taiwan was “disappointed and dissatisfied” and had “repeatedly expressed serious concern” over the matter, the foreign ministry said on its website. It added that representations had been lodged with the South Korean government, urging an immediate correction, but no positive response had been received.
The “unfriendly listing” had caused “unnecessary confusion” and inconvenience for Taiwanese travellers and also “hurt public sentiment” in Taiwan, the statement said.
Beijing views Taiwan as part of China, to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the United States and its treaty ally Japan, do not recognise the self-governed island as an independent state. But Washington is opposed to any attempt to seize Taiwan by force and remains committed to supplying it with weapons.
On Friday, the Korean foreign ministry told Taiwan’s Central News Agency that Seoul would take multiple factors into consideration and continue consulting with relevant authorities regarding the listing. It added that Seoul continued to uphold its consistent stance of promoting ongoing, non-official substantive cooperation with Taiwan.
Beijing’s foreign ministry has not commented on the issue.
The controversy comes amid the ongoing China-Japan diplomatic row sparked by Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s remarks on November 7. She told parliament that a hypothetical People’s Liberation Army attack on Taiwan could prompt a military response from Tokyo – crossing what Beijing calls its “red line” on the sensitive issue.

Beijing has responded with sharp criticism and a series of retaliatory steps, including suspending imports of Japanese seafood and issuing travel and study warnings, and has also repeatedly raised the issue at the United Nations.
On Wednesday, South Korean President Lee Jae-myung said that Seoul could play a mediating role to ease the tensions “where possible”, while emphasising that taking sides would only escalate the dispute.
South Korea rolled out the e-arrival system in February to gradually replace paper arrival cards, which it plans to phase out by the end of the year. Travellers must fill out the electronic form up to 72 hours before arrival.
The tensions with China have taken a toll on Japan’s tourism industry, with the Chinese travel warnings triggering widespread cancellations of flights, and hotel and tour bookings.
Mainland China is one of the biggest sources of foreign tourists to Japan, accounting for 7.48 million trips in the first nine months of this year, according to official Japanese data.
Since the warnings from Beijing, Chinese tourists seeking alternative destinations have been looking at South Korea, Thailand and Europe, including Russia, with increased interest.
Russian President Vladimir Putin on Monday signed a decree granting visa-free access to Russia for up to 30 days for many categories of Chinese citizens, mirroring Beijing’s move in effect since September.

data.title: Under Current movie review: Aaron Kwok, Francis Ng seek justice in fun but silly thriller
data.description:
2.5/5 stars
Under Current is a quintessential example of the Hong Kong crime film in its present, transitional state. While it paints a mostly watchable portrait of greed and corruption, the film’s story also happens to be thoroughly detached from reality – music to the ears of censors, perhaps.
Directed by Alan Mak Siu-fai (Infernal Affairs) from a screenplay he co-scripted with Lam Fung, this thematic follow-up to Mak’s 2019 anti-corruption drama Integrity again takes a distinctly convoluted narrative to ponder the state of law enforcement, only to lose much of its conviction with preposterous twists and turns.
It begins in sensational fashion at the gala of a major charity organisation, whose CFO, Yeung To (Simon Yam Tat-wah), suddenly appears hanging above the stage – with the words “Clear my name” written on his shirt – while its president, Ko Shing-man (Alex Fong Chung-sun), is giving a speech below.


It immediately becomes clear to the audience that Ko is a pantomime villain involved in a range of criminal activities, from embezzlement to money laundering, and he is trying to locate HK$200 million (US$26 million) that has recently vanished from the charity funds amid the chaos of persistent malpractice.
Meanwhile, police sergeant Or Ting-pong (Francis Ng Chun-yu), working from a tip-off and without a search warrant, is secretly investigating charity accounts entrusted to the law firm of barrister Ma Ying-fung (Aaron Kwok Fu-shing). When discovered, Or astonishingly suggests that Ma simply hand the files over.
Casually incorporating these abuses of power on both sides of the law into its overblown drama, Under Current follows Ma – also a taekwondo master – and Or’s dogged attempt to expose the full extent of corruption at the charity, which turns out to extend to drug trafficking and murder.

Conveniently, Ma is just the perfect candidate for this irregular quest. His righteousness was forged when he was orphaned in a childhood incident exacerbated by the indifference of bystanders; it is reignited after he secures an acquittal in a rape case on a technicality, only for the complainant to kill herself afterwards.
A rather bizarre attempt to ponder the individual’s moral responsibilities, Mak’s film shows a deep distrust of the legal system – presumably full of loopholes to be exploited – and even quotes the Nuremberg principles in its mangled logic to condemn the system’s lawful participants.
Ultimately, this most superficial account of justice and conscience is only leavened by its occasional action scenes – mostly involving German Cheung Man-kit’s assassin – and few stand-out supporting characters, notably David Chiang Da-wei as Ma’s mentor and Felix Lok Ying-kwan as a shady, menacing kingpin.

Under Current is so relaxed with the way things are in its fictional little world that it does not once care to mention Hong Kong’s Independent Commission Against Corruption (ICAC).
Want more articles like this? Follow SCMP Film on Facebook

data.title: Phetjeeja says potential Rodrigues fight has makings of ‘classic like Rodtang v Superlek’
data.description:
Ahead of her return to Muay Thai against Martyna Dominczak on Saturday, ONE women’s atomweight kickboxing world champion Phetjeeja Lukjaoporongtom has said her long-targeted showdown with Allycia Hellen Rodrigues could deliver a modern classic.
The Thai star told the Post that the match-up at ONE Fight Night 38 had the potential to become the female equivalent of Superlek v Rodtang, a rivalry that defined ONE’s Muay Thai landscape in recent years.
Before she can chase that superfight, however, the 23-year-old must first overcome Poland’s Dominczak.
The rising contender is 3-1 in ONE and unbeaten since dropping to atomweight, but she enters the contest with far less ring time than Phetjeeja, who has amassed more than 200 career wins and even defeated Stamp Fairtex on points at age 15.
Returning to Muay Thai after almost two years has provided Phetjeeja with renewed motivation.
“I’m very happy to be back in Muay Thai,” she told the Post. “It has been, I think, almost two years since I last fought Muay Thai and I’m very excited to be fighting in the rules that I’m good at.”

Although her experience level far exceeds Dominczak’s, Phetjeeja refuses to underestimate the Polish fighter.
“I think she has fought in ONE maybe three or four times. I think she’s a good fighter and I think she will prepare herself for this fight very well, just like me,” she said. “I think she would just come at 100 per cent, so I think she is ready.”
Phetjeeja then shared a technical breakdown of her opponent’s skills.
“Her strength is that she is a very well-rounded fighter,” she said. “She has punches, she has kicks, she also uses push kicks very well, so I think I have to be careful with that and elbows and knees. For me, I don’t think there is any weakness that I see until now.”
The biggest storyline, however, surrounds a potential showdown with Rodrigues, the reigning atomweight Muay Thai champion who has struggled to find opponents at her level.
Fans have been calling for the match-up for more than two years and Phetjeeja admitted that she had expected to face Rodrigues.
“Yes, I also thought I would be facing Allycia. I’m just not sure what happened,” she said.
She added that from her own perspective, “Maybe I’ll need one or two more Thai fights first. Maybe after I fight with Martyna and the result is good, maybe the next one would be Allycia.”
Given the shallow depth of the atomweight Muay Thai division, she believes the winner of this bout should move directly into title contention.
“Well I think it’s possible – the winner to get the title shot because there are fewer female fighters in this division and Allycia is also like it’s hard to find someone to match her level,” she said.
Phetjeeja insisted she was not focused on whether she was the fighter who could finally end Rodrigues’ reign, but she did promise fireworks if the bout finally happened.
“I’m not sure about that [ending Rodrigues’ reign] but what I’m sure is that if I have a chance to face her, I think it would be one of the most entertaining matches ever,” she said.
She then made the comparison that will excite fans.
“I think it would be a modern classic like Rodtang versus Superlek,” she said. “I’ve been calling Allycia out for maybe, I think, two years and I think it will be the fight that a lot of people are looking forward to and I am looking forward to it too.”


Phetjeeja also reflected on her lone meeting with Fairtex, which took place when she was still a teenager.
“I faced Stamp only one time. I think I was 15 or 16 at that time. We were fighting in her hometown in Rayong. I won by points,” she said.
A rematch remains possible. “I think it would be great. Maybe there’s a chance for us to face each other. If there’s a chance I’m happy to do it,” she added.
With ONE Fight Night 38 looming, Phetjeeja said she was happy with her training camp since returning to Team Mehdi Zatout at Venum.
“Coming back to Venum, it just feels familiar to me and it allows me to get back into my style,” she said. “My preparation has been good. I am at 100 per cent. I’m ready to go.”
A victory over Dominczak would place her closer than ever to the superfight she has been chasing. And if her prediction proves accurate, Phetjeeja vs Rodrigues may become the next great rivalry of this era in women’s Muay Thai.

data.title: ‘Breasts will be larger’: China expectant dads voice discomfort over parenting app advice
data.description:
A leading parenting platform in China has sparked discomfort among expectant fathers with feeds such as “her body has become the shape of S” and “you can have some sex at this stage,” leading many to voice their complaints on social media.
An internet user, whose wife is 22 weeks pregnant, shared that both he and his wife are registered members of Baby Tree, an app that caters to families with pregnant women and infants.
According to the Xiaoxiang Morning Herald, the couple regularly receive messages from the app following their pregnancy update. While the content generally focuses on the physical changes of the expectant mother, some recent messages have made the man uneasy.

On November 25, he received a particular message stating: “Her breasts will possibly be larger. Her body has taken on an ‘S’ shape, reflecting a strong femininity. This will surprise her.”
He criticised this messaging, asking: “Why does it describe a pregnant woman like this? Will a woman at this stage care about the so-called ‘S’ curve?”
On November 27, another message from Baby Tree read: “The fetus develops stably. You can have some sex without any concerns. This can enhance the bond between you two.”
The following day, the feed continued: “She may have lacked interest in sex before, but her attitude might now change.”
The man noted that he and his wife were focused solely on maintaining a healthy diet and regular sleep, rather than on intimacy.

“She feels pain in many parts of her body. Would you be so insensitive to have sex with her?” he questioned, expressing a desire for the platform to provide more information about pregnant women’s emotional needs.
A customer service representative from Baby Tree explained that these feeds are generated automatically by an artificial intelligence tool.
“If you don’t find them useful, you can opt out of receiving the feeds. However, the messages are general advice, not targeted advocacy or malicious content,” the employee clarified.
Another staff member added that the messages are tailored for all registered users, varying by the expectant mother’s stage in pregnancy.

“Our aim is to keep couples informed about the overall body changes of the pregnant woman at different stages. These messages are not solely focused on sex,” the second employee stated.
The story has captivated mainland Chinese social media, with opinions sharply divided.
One woman remarked: “These messages are disgusting. I will not allow my husband to register for this app to receive such feeds.”
Conversely, another individual defended the app, stating: “He is being overly critical. I don’t see any issue with those messages.”

data.title: Is the Philippines vulnerable to another military-triggered political crisis?
data.description:
Allegations that a small group of retired generals is trying to push the Philippines military to abandon President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr have intensified concerns about how susceptible the armed forces are to political pressure and extra-constitutional manoeuvres.
Observers told This Week in Asia the retired officers appear to be drawing inspiration from the 2001 “EDSA Dos” uprising – when mass protests and a unified military command helped force President Joseph Estrada from office – but argued such a formula is unlikely to work today given the military’s stronger constitutional ethos and the far different political environment.
In September, military chief General Romeo Brawner Jnr disclosed that he had rejected a direct appeal from eight retired generals and colonels who visited military headquarters urging him to confront corruption and consider withdrawing support from Marcos – a move senior security sources described as a bid for a “soft coup” that would pave the way for Vice-President Sara Duterte-Carpio.
Last month, Brawner said the armed forces’ intelligence community was now investigating reports that retired officers and several government officials were involved in destabilisation efforts.


“When there are supposed destabilisation plots, of course, we will look into it seriously,” he told reporters on November 15, adding that investigators were already examining the claims.
Risk analyst Ronald Llamas, a former adviser to the late president Benigno Aquino III, said the generals were “using EDSA Dos as their template”, convinced that a coordinated withdrawal of military support could again trigger a change in leadership.
What happened during ‘EDSA Dos’
EDSA Dos refers to a three-day uprising in January 2001, when mass anger over a stalled impeachment case against President Joseph Estrada, who had been accused of corruption and taking illegal gambling payoffs, swelled into huge street protests in Manila.
The demonstrations centred on Epifanio de los Santos Avenue, or EDSA, a major highway that has become the symbolic site of Philippine people-power movements. The 2001 protests were therefore dubbed EDSA Dos, or the second EDSA uprising, following the 1986 revolt that toppled Ferdinand Marcos Snr.
The mass unrest convinced the military that the crisis had reached a constitutional breaking point. General Angelo Reyes, then the head of the armed forces, later recalled that the scale and intensity of public outrage made the outcome clear.
“To me, the answer was simple, the outcome certain [when] the outrage of the people is genuine, deep-seated, all said, the people will win,” he was quoted as saying in the book Hot Money, Warm Bodies by Australian journalist Greg Hutchinson.
Believing the moment required a unified institutional response, Reyes and the military’s service commanders agreed to withdraw support from Estrada. Appearing before crowds at EDSA on January 19, he announced: “On behalf of the 113,000-strong Armed Forces of the Philippines, we wish to announce that we are withdrawing our support from the incumbent president.”

“We will support his constitutionally mandated successor under Article 2, Section 3, of the Constitution … the AFP must protect the people and the State,” he said, citing the military’s role as “protector of the People and the State” under the 1987 Constitution.
Llamas said today’s retired generals are attempting to emulate this moment, hoping a single, unified withdrawal led by the military chief would again force a transition.
But he noted that the bloody 1989 coup attempts – a series of rebellions by rogue officers trying to topple President Corazon Aquino – had left the military traumatised after more than three hundred soldiers died fighting one another. Any move they contemplate today, he said, would therefore be limited to a collective, non-violent action – if it happened at all.
A changed landscape
Analysts said that despite the historical echoes, the political and institutional landscape is now far removed from 2001, with the military being more professionalised, the public less tolerant of extra-constitutional manoeuvres and political factions far more fragmented than during the Estrada era.
When asked whether retired generals could sway current military chief Brawner, Orlando Mercado, professor of praxis at the Ateneo School of Government, said the conditions for a successful military-led transition no longer exist.
Unlike Myanmar and Thailand, where coups are entrenched, “liberal democracy is now deeply rooted” in the Philippines, he said.
He added that “the deeply rooted ethos of the Philippine military today is the faithful respect for the Constitution”.
Mercado has first-hand experience. As defence secretary during EDSA Dos, he stood beside Reyes when the military broke with Estrada. Even then, he said, he had been “very disconcerted” by the “volume and the type of evidence” emerging against the president, whom he had known as a friend and Senate colleague.
He conceded that the scandal engulfing Marcos’ administration – involving billions of pesos in poorly built and outright non-existent “ghost” flood control projects and triggering waves of public protests – echoed the conditions of that earlier crisis, describing it as “corruption on steroids”.
However, he said the current defence secretary, Gilberto Teodoro Jnr, “was correct in saying [the military] has no business participating in political questions. That’s not our job. And I think that has been well received by the [military] command.”
Succession mechanics
Another key figure from EDSA Dos said that while the military’s withdrawal of support was decisive in forcing Estrada from office, it was not the sole reason his vice-president, Gloria Macapagal Arroyo, was ultimately able to assume the presidency.
Antonio Carpio, a retired Supreme Court associate justice who at the time was advising Arroyo privately, said the crucial turning point came when the entire military abandoned Estrada, which “legally incapacitated” him and activated the constitutional line of succession.

He emphasised that Arroyo did not take office because the armed forces declared her president but because the Constitution required a successor once a sitting president could no longer govern.
Carpio recalled warning her of the danger of relying solely on military backing. “I told Arroyo then that if she became president [by virtue of the military], you are now under military rule. So you have to follow the [constitutional succession] and the military will be under you.”
To formalise that process, he drafted a letter for Arroyo requesting that the Supreme Court administer her oath on the grounds that Estrada was “permanently incapacitated” following the military and police withdrawal.
The Court initially intended to swear her in only as “acting president”. But Carpio disclosed that Associate Justice Reynato Panganiban warned Chief Justice Hilario Davide Jnr that using the term “acting” before the emotionally charged EDSA crowd could provoke violence: “If you tell the crowd that Arroyo is being sworn in as ‘acting president’, they will throw things at you and maybe fatally harm you.” Davide then swore her in simply as “president”.
Estrada later insisted, “I never resigned”, prompting a constitutional dispute, but the Supreme Court ruled he had effectively undergone a “constructive resignation”.
Carpio said he doubted a similar sequence of events could unfold today, beginning with a military withdrawal of support. For one, “the military doesn’t want Sara” Duterte-Carpio to be president because of her perceived pro-China leanings.
Llamas echoed this, saying her “pro-China stance” is deeply unpopular within the armed forces, which has been on the front line of Manila’s confrontations with Beijing in the South China Sea. “The military is not pro-China” and knows that “any move to destabilise Marcos, China will be the beneficiary”, he added.

data.title: China’s Xiaomi says returns from AI investments ‘far exceed expectations’
data.description:
Chinese tech firm Xiaomi reaped returns from its artificial intelligence spending in 2025 that “far exceeded expectations”, the company’s president Lu Weibing said in a recorded video posted on Chinese social media platform Douyin.
Lu said the Beijing-based company was pivoting to embodied AI after heavy investment in general AI over the past few quarters. The Beijing-based company’s efforts to apply AI in electric vehicles (EVs) and robotics are similar to Elon Musk’s plan of using the advanced technology in Tesla cars and Optimus humanoids.
“Our progress in AI large models and applications has far exceeded our expectations,” Lu said on Wednesday, adding that the company believed the deep integration of AI with the physical world represented the next generation of intelligent technology.
The smartphone and EV maker launched its first AI model, MiMo, in April. It recently open-sourced MiMo-Embodied, a model that demonstrated “state-of-the-art performance in both autonomous driving and embodied AI tasks”, according to Xiaomi.

China has seen a surge of interest in the adoption of AI in the EV sector. Xiaomi debuted its premium SU7 Ultra in February, integrating the company’s Hyper-Autonomous Driving technology, a smart driving system.
Interest has also grown in embodied AI, the integration of the technology into physical systems. Xiaomi ramped up investment in the robotics sector following the debut of its robot dog in 2021 and a humanoid prototype in 2022.
Lu emphasised that Xiaomi was “hungry for talent”. The company’s MiMo team recently hired Luo Fuli, a former researcher at DeepSeek who was a key contributor to the Chinese AI start-up’s V2 model. Luo is scheduled to speak at Xiaomi’s developer conference on December 17, as head of the MiMo team.
Xiaomi’s smart EV, AI and other new initiatives turned a profit for the first time in the third quarter. The businesses collectively generated a record 29 billion yuan (US$4.1 billion) in revenue in the quarter, up 199 per cent year on year. Xiaomi’s total revenue for the quarter increased 22 per cent year on year to 113.1 billion yuan.
The company’s adjusted net profit in the three months ended September 30 reached 11.3 billion yuan, according to its earnings report on Tuesday, while revenue for the quarter rose 22.3 per cent to 113.1 billion yuan.

data.title: K-pop girl group Twice to proceed with Hong Kong concert this weekend
data.description:
Korean girl group Twice will go ahead with its concert at the 50,000-seat Kai Tak Stadium over the weekend, despite several others being cancelled or postponed after Hong Kong’s most fatal fire in decades.
The concert organiser on Friday also said it would donate part of its revenue from ticket sales to support relief measures for fire victims, without specifying the amount.
The K-pop group’s management company JYP Entertainment announced on social media that Twice would press on with the concerts over the weekend, their first in the city.
A Kai Tak Stadium spokeswoman said on Friday that attendees would be allowed to enter the stadium from 11.30am on Saturday, six and a half hours before the concert began at 6pm.
JYP Entertainment said it had “considered carefully and discussed in-depth whether to go ahead with the event at such a painful moment” and was “monitoring the situation together with organiser Live Nation”.
“Given it was a decision made in such a difficult situation, we will solemnly prepare for and execute the performance.”
The deadly fire that broke out in Tai Po’s Wang Fuk Court last week has claimed at least 159 lives, including a firefighter’s, and left an estimated 4,000 people homeless.
Some ticket holders said they had lost their concert tickets in the fire and asked Live Nation and Cityline (Hong Kong) to issue new tickets, according to the two companies’ Instagram accounts.
JYP Entertainment earlier donated HK$2 million (US$257,000) to the charity World Vision Hong Kong to provide post-disaster relief and emergency aid to fire victims.
Live Nation said on Thursday that, together with the band, it would devote a proportion of proceeds from ticket sales to support relief and recovery efforts.
“Our thoughts remain with the entire community during this difficult time,” it said.
Twice will perform two concerts at Kai Tak Stadium on Saturday and Sunday.
The events are a part of the group’s “This is For” world tour. Their last stop before arriving in Hong Kong was Kaohsiung in late November.
Several concerts and events in Hong Kong were cancelled or postponed in the wake of the fire.
Local singer Karen Mok Man-wai cancelled her performance scheduled for December 20.
Emperor Entertainment Group also postponed several events featuring singers it represented, including Angela Hui Ching-wan and Ken Hung Cheuk-lap, that were originally planned for late November.

data.title: TikTok will block under-16s in Australia to comply with ‘upsetting’ new law
data.description:
TikTok said on Friday it will comply with Australia’s imminent ban on under-16s joining social media on the day it comes into force, but told users the changes “may be upsetting”.
Australia’s world-first legislation comes into effect December 10, curbing the world’s most popular social media platforms and websites, including TikTok, Instagram and YouTube.
Companies face fines of A$49.5 million (US$32 million) if they fail to take “reasonable steps” to comply.
TikTok will block under-16s in Australia on the day the law comes into effect, it said, meaning they will no longer be able to hold or create an account.
“Teens with an existing account will be notified that they will no longer be able to use their existing account, which will become inactive,” it said.

“If they previously published content, it will no longer be available for others to view on TikTok.”
The social media giant said youngsters who have been blocked can submit an appeal to prove their age, including by facial images, credit card authorisation or official ID.
“We understand that these changes may be upsetting, but they are necessary to ensure that TikTok complies with Australian law,” the company said in a statement.
Teens who fall under the law will have a choice of confirming their age, downloading their information, deleting their account, or asking for a reminder to recover their TikTok accounts when they turn 16.
TikTok urged parents to “have conversations” with their teens to ensure they are truthful about their age.


A week ahead of the ban, Communications Minister Anika Wells said some Australian teens had killed themselves as social media algorithms “latched on” – targeting them with content that drained their self-esteem.
“This specific law will not fix every harm occurring on the internet, but it will make it easier for kids to chase a better version of themselves,” she told reporters.
An internet rights group last week launched a legal challenge to halt the ban.
The Digital Freedom Project said it had challenged the laws in Australia’s High Court, calling them an “unfair” assault on freedom of speech.
Australia’s restrictions have generated interest around the world as regulators wrestle with the potential dangers of social media.
Malaysia indicated it was planning to block children under 16 from signing up to social media accounts next year, while New Zealand will introduce a similar ban.

data.title: From steel to space: China giant sells first 2 satellites as bold pivot bears fruit
data.description:
Jigang Group spent decades forging the metal for China’s infrastructure boom. Now, the former industrial titan is building hardware for the final frontier.
The state-owned company, once one of the nation’s top steel producers, has secured orders for its first two satellites – an all-important step in its years-long transformation to capture demand in the country’s burgeoning commercial space industry.
Reporting this week on the purchase, the Economic Observer said production was expected to begin this month at the company’s new satellite-assembly base in Jinan, the provincial capital of Shandong. No buyer names were given, and it was unclear if multiple buyers were involved.
“In 2017, Jigang Group – once China’s seventh-largest steel producer – shut down its furnaces, phased out 10 million tonnes of production capacity, and embarked on a path of transformation,” Zhao Liang, the project head at the firm’s subsidiary, the Jinan Satellite Industry Development Group, told the paper.
Years in the making, the transition was marked by a 450 million yuan (US$62 million) investment in 2023 to establish the satellite-assembly base and position the firm in the aerospace supply chain.
Three months ago, the Jinan government said the base was set for production. Authorities noted that it could already produce up to 20 satellites a year, each weighing about 500kg (1,100lbs).
“Within three years, its output is expected to reach 100 satellites per year, supporting the deployment and replenishment of commercial satellite constellations,” the official statement said on September 8. “The base’s core capabilities lie in assembly, environmental testing and satellite electrical testing, encompassing storage, fault handling and comprehensive testing and data processing.”


As Jigang Group tries to secure enough sales to fully utilise its new production capabilities, the move reflects a broader push into the commercial space sector, driven by supportive industrial policies and local investments.
As of November, China had more than 90,000 space-related companies, with nearly 60 per cent founded in the past three years, according to corporate database Qichacha.
The 500kg class of “small” satellites is a segment with relatively low financial and technical barriers. Their compact size, simpler production processes and more flexible launch options make them more accessible and affordable for a wide range of companies and institutions.
The industry surpassed a market value of 1 trillion yuan (US$141 billion) in 2020 and has since maintained steady growth, with a compound annual growth rate of around 22 per cent, according to an analysis by the China Centre for Information Industry Development, as cited by Xinhua.
That would put the market value of China’s commercial space industry at 2.8 trillion yuan (US$396.2 billion) for 2025, according to the centre, which is under China’s Ministry of Industry and Information Technology.
In comparison, the global commercial space market is projected to exceed US$700 billion by 2025, according to forecasts cited by the Economic Information Daily.
To manage such rapid growth, the China National Space Administration recently established a new department dedicated to overseeing the commercial space sector, Xinhua reported on Saturday.
With Jigang’s new satellite-assembly base in Jinan geared to go, it has partnered with local authorities to establish an investment fund to lure suppliers, project head Zhao told the Economic Observer.
According to the report, the company’s satellites could address market demands across a range of sectors, including agriculture and rural development, urban governance, smart water management, emergency response, transport, and national defence and military affairs.
Other companies, from automotive to telecommunications, are also entering the fray.
Geely Technology Group – a unit of Geely, one of China’s leading carmakers – says on its website that its “world’s first” factory for mass-producing satellites in Zhejiang province combines aerospace and automotive mass-production techniques.
The facility, dubbed a “satellite super factory”, is said to have shortened satellite-production cycles to under 28 days and reduced costs by about 45 per cent, with an annual output of up to 500 units.
